name: Linux Folio and Multi-size THP
summary: Two parallel Linux kernel efforts (folio refactoring 2022+, mTHP 2023+) that reshape the OS-level memory substrate every userspace allocator sits on: explicit-size folios replacing ambiguous `struct page`, plus intermediate huge-page sizes filling the 4 KiB-to-2 MiB gap.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Every userspace allocator in chapter 7 sits on top of OS page allocation. Two parallel Linux kernel efforts have reshaped that substrate in 2022-2026 and matter for any language runtime targeting Linux: the folio refactoring of `struct page` and multi-size Transparent Huge Pages.

The runtime-design implication is unified: allocator designs that explicitly cooperate with the OS page-size hierarchy (4 KiB to multi-size mTHP to 2 MiB THP to 1 GiB hugepages) outperform allocators that assume one page size everywhere. TCMalloc + Temeraire is the production existence proof; jemalloc has hugepage hints in the same direction.

sections.title: Folio refactoring (Linux 5.16+, 2022+)
sections.content:
Matthew Wilcox et al.'s folio refactoring (merged starting Linux 5.16, 2022) replaces uses of `struct page` (the kernel's per-page metadata) with `struct folio` — a typed handle representing a contiguous physically-aligned run of pages. Where `struct page` conflated single-page and multi-page (compound) cases ambiguously, requiring runtime checks throughout the kernel, `struct folio` makes the typing explicit at compile time.

The 2025 milestone is moving `struct folio` to be allocated independently of `struct page`, eliminating a long-standing memory-management ambiguity. The lesson for language runtime designers: the OS-level page metadata you allocate on top of is moving from "single page or maybe compound" to "explicit-size folio," which makes huge-page-aware allocators cleaner to implement.

sections.title: Multi-size Transparent Huge Pages (Linux 6.6+, 2023+)
sections.content:
Multi-size Transparent Huge Pages (mTHP) (merged Linux 6.6+, 2023+) extends Transparent Huge Pages (THP) — which previously gave only PMD-sized 2 MiB pages — to allocate folios of *intermediate* sizes (16 KiB, 32 KiB, 64 KiB, 128 KiB, 256 KiB, 512 KiB, 1 MiB) called "anonymous mTHP." This is critical because the binary choice between 4 KiB pages (no TLB benefit) and 2 MiB pages (memory waste, allocation pressure) was poor for many workloads. mTHP fills the middle.

UEK8 documentation reports 5-20% performance improvements on mixed workloads from mTHP enablement; ARM64 work in Linux 6.17 (2025) optimised khugepaged for batched PTE operations on large folios. Status (as of 2026-04): merged and enabled in modern distros; allocator-level adoption is still rolling out, with TCMalloc + Temeraire and jemalloc-with-hugepage-hints the early adopters.

sections.title: Implications for allocator design
sections.content: For new language runtimes, exposing explicit large-allocation paths (such as `std.mem.Allocator` policy hints in Zig or allocator zones in C3) lets runtime code request mTHP-friendly sizes when allocation patterns warrant. The era of "every allocation is 4 KiB-page-backed and the runtime ignores hugepages" is ending; new language allocator APIs (see the chapter 7.12 records on Zig, Odin, C3, and the rest) sit at the right abstraction layer to surface page-size policy without baking it into the language.
